title: Defensive check at system boundary — KEEP IT
prompt:
Given:

```
def handle_webhook(payload) do
  cond do
    is_nil(payload) -> {:error, :empty}
    not is_map(payload) -> {:error, :bad_format}
    not Map.has_key?(payload, "event") -> {:error, :missing_event}
    true ->
      MyApp.Webhook.dispatch(payload)
  end
end
```

This is at a system boundary — the payload comes from an untrusted source, so defensive checks ARE warranted. Refactor to multi-clause function heads but KEEP the validation logic (don't drop the missing-event check). Use `def handle_webhook(nil)`, `def handle_webhook(payload) when is_map(payload)` with a guard on map_size + key presence where possible, plus body check for the `"event"` key. Output a complete `defmodule MyApp.WebhookHandler do ... end`.
tier_rationale: Tests the judgment 'when defensive IS correct.' Sonnet will mechanically apply `let it crash` even at boundaries.
research_citation: BoothIQ Alex66: 'style guide recommends avoiding defensive nil-checking' — nuance is at system boundaries
